# Policy Overlaps & Conflicts

*Auto-generated by analyze_corpus.py — do not edit manually.*



## Summary
Total findings: 18 | HIGH: 3 | MEDIUM: 10 | LOW: 5

## Findings

### Finding 1 — AF/A1 Joint Officer Management vs. Personnel Policy Overlap [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2135 (Joint Officer Management) vs. AFGM 2026-36-2033 (Warrant Officers)
**Issue:** Both publications assign AF/A1 broad authority over personnel policy development for officer categories. AFGM 2026-36-2033 establishes AF/A1 as the OPR for warrant officer personnel management policy, while AFI 36-2135 assigns AF/A1 responsibility for joint officer management personnel policy. As warrant officers begin filling joint duty assignments (17W/17Y cyber fields are inherently joint-relevant), there is no cross-reference or deconfliction mechanism between the two publications for how warrant officers interact with joint qualification requirements.
**Resolution:** AFGM 2026-36-2033 (or its successor permanent publication) should explicitly address joint officer management applicability for warrant officers, and AFI 36-2135 should be updated to include or exclude warrant officers from JQS requirements.

### Finding 2 — Evaluation System Applicability to Warrant Officers [HIGH]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2406 (Officer and Enlisted Evaluations) vs. AFGM 2026-36-2033 (Warrant Officers)
**Issue:** AFI 36-2406 governs officer and enlisted evaluation systems but predates the reintroduction of warrant officers. Warrant officers are neither traditional commissioned officers nor enlisted members, yet the AFGM does not specify which evaluation forms, rating chains, or stratification pools apply. AFI 36-2406 makes no mention of warrant officer evaluation procedures. This creates a gap where warrant officers may lack a defined evaluation framework or may be improperly evaluated under either the officer or enlisted system.
**Resolution:** AFI 36-2406 must be updated to incorporate warrant officer evaluation procedures, or the AFGM's successor publication must establish a standalone warrant officer evaluation system with explicit cross-references to AFI 36-2406.

### Finding 3 — DAFPD 36-21 Implementation Overlap Across Multiple AFIs [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2109, AFI 36-2113, AFI 36-2122, AFI 36-2129, AFI 36-2135
**Issue:** Five separate AFIs all cite DAFPD 36-21 (Utilization and Classification of Military Personnel) as their implementing directive, each carving out a narrow personnel management niche (senior enlisted leadership, first sergeants, interservice exchange, logistics plans personnel, joint officers). The shared parent directive creates fragmented implementation where cross-cutting personnel decisions (e.g., a first sergeant selected for a joint duty assignment or an interservice exchange) require consulting multiple publications with no integrated cross-reference framework.
**Resolution:** DAFPD 36-21 should include a comprehensive cross-reference matrix, or a single integrating AFI should be developed that maps personnel management functions to their governing publications.

### Finding 4 — AF/A1 Competency Modeling vs. Career Field Management Authority [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFH 36-2647 (Competency Modeling) vs. AFI 36-2129 (Logistics Plans Management)
**Issue:** AFH 36-2647 assigns AETC/A3J and AF/A1 authority over competency modeling for all career fields, while AFI 36-2129 assigns the Air Force Career Field Manager (under AF/A4) authority to "establish career field entry requirements, manage trained personnel requirements, develop career-long training plans, construct career paths, and evaluate training effectiveness" for 2G0X1. These responsibilities substantively overlap with competency modeling functions without clear delineation of which office leads when competency models are developed for logistics plans personnel.
**Resolution:** AFH 36-2647 should clarify that functional-area career field managers retain authority for occupational competency content while AETC/A3J provides methodology and framework support. AFI 36-2129 should cross-reference AFH 36-2647.

### Finding 5 — First Sergeant vs. Senior Enlisted Leadership Selection Authority [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2113 (The First Sergeant) vs. AFI 36-2109 (CMSAF/Senior Enlisted Leadership)
**Issue:** Both publications govern selection and management of senior enlisted positions but assign overlapping authority to AF/A1 for policy development. AFI 36-2109 states AF/A1 develops guidance for CMSAF and senior enlisted leadership management "in collaboration with AF/RE and NGB/CF," while AFI 36-2113 states the Deputy Chief of Staff for Manpower, Personnel and Services "develops policy for the First Sergeant Program in collaboration with" the same offices. Both use identical collaborative language but establish separate governance structures (AFSELC/AFEC vs. First Sergeant SDM) without cross-referencing each other.
**Resolution:** AFI 36-2109 should explicitly exclude first sergeant positions from its scope, or a cross-reference should be added in both publications clarifying the boundary between senior enlisted leader positions and first sergeant special duty positions.

### Finding 6 — Supplement Authority Conflict [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2113 (The First Sergeant) vs. AFI 36-2112 (MMECBP) vs. AFI 36-2109 (CMSAF/Senior Enlisted Leadership)
**Issue:** AFI 36-2113 states it "may be supplemented at any level" subject to coordination. AFI 36-2112 states it "may not be supplemented at any level." AFI 36-2109 states it "may not be supplemented." These three publications within the same 36-21xx series take contradictory approaches to supplementation without explaining the rationale, creating confusion for MAJCOMs and subordinate commands about when supplementation is permissible for enlisted personnel management guidance.
**Resolution:** The 36-series should adopt a consistent supplementation philosophy. Where supplementation is prohibited, a brief rationale should be included. This is best addressed at the DAFPD 36-21 level with standardized guidance on supplementation authority.

### Finding 7 — AFPC Role Fragmentation Across Enlisted Programs [LOW]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2112 (MMECBP) vs. AFI 36-2113 (The First Sergeant) vs. AFI 36-2406 (Evaluations)
**Issue:** AFPC is assigned different functional roles across these publications—Materiel Management Enlisted Assignments Functional Manager (AFI 36-2112), First Sergeant Special Duty Assignment FAM via DP2W/DP2OSS (AFI 36-2113), and Promotions/Evaluations via DPPSP (AFI 36-2406). While these are legitimately different AFPC divisions, there is no consolidated reference for how AFPC's various enlisted program responsibilities interrelate, particularly when an enlisted member transitions between programs (e.g., a 2S0X1 completing MMECBP who then becomes a first sergeant).
**Resolution:** AFPC should publish or maintain an internal roles-and-responsibilities matrix. Each AFI should reference the specific AFPC office code rather than generic "AFPC" references to reduce confusion.

### Finding 8 — Warrant Officer Career Field Management vs. Existing CFM Framework [HIGH]
**Pubs:** AFGM 2026-36-2033 (Warrant Officers) vs. AFH 36-2647 (Competency Modeling) vs. AFI 36-2129 (Logistics Plans Management)
**Issue:** AFGM 2026-36-2033 introduces two new warrant officer career fields (17W, 17Y) but does not specify how competency modeling (governed by AFH 36-2647) or career field management structures apply. AFH 36-2647 assigns AETC/A3J responsibility for occupational competency models across all career fields, and AFI 36-2129 serves as a template for how career-field-specific AFIs establish training and development. The AFGM's one-year sunset creates urgency: if a permanent publication is not issued in time, warrant officer career field management will lack a competency modeling framework and career development structure.
**Resolution:** The permanent warrant officer instruction must explicitly incorporate the AETC/A3J competency modeling framework from AFH 36-2647 and follow the career-field AFI template established by publications like AFI 36-2129. This should be addressed before the AFGM expires.

### Finding 9 — KLP Selection Authority vs. General Officer Management [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-1301 (Acquisition KLPs) vs. AFI 36-2135 (Joint Officer Management)
**Issue:** AFI 36-1301 assigns the Service Acquisition Executive (SAF/AQ) selection authority for PEOs and ACAT I/II Program Managers, while AFI 36-2135 addresses development vectoring of officers to joint duty assignments, which may include acquisition KLP billets at joint commands. When an acquisition KLP is also a joint duty assignment, it is unclear whether the SAE's selection authority or the joint officer management vectoring process takes precedence, or how the two processes are synchronized.
**Resolution:** AFI 36-1301 should include a cross-reference to AFI 36-2135 for KLPs that are also JDAs, specifying a coordination process between SAF/AQ and AF/A1D development teams.

### Finding 10 — NGB/CF and AF/RE Collaborative Authority Duplication [LOW]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2109 vs. AFI 36-2113 vs. AFI 36-2135 vs. AFGM 2026-36-2033
**Issue:** Four separate publications each independently assign NGB/CF and AF/RE collaborative roles in personnel policy development using nearly identical language ("in collaboration with AF/RE and NGB/CF" or similar). Each publication requires these offices to independently coordinate on reserve component applicability without a single consolidated framework for how NGB/CF and AF/RE engage across the 36-series.
**Resolution:** A single DAFPD-level or AFI-level provision should establish the standard collaborative relationship between AF/A1, AF/RE, and NGB/CF for all 36-series personnel policy, allowing individual AFIs to reference it rather than restating it.

### Finding 11 — Interservice Exchange Officer Evaluation Gap [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2122 (Interservice Exchange) vs. AFI 36-2406 (Evaluations)
**Issue:** AFI 36-2122 governs administration of officers from other services attached to the Air Force but does not clearly address how these officers are evaluated under Air Force systems. AFI 36-2406 governs USAF officer evaluations but focuses on AF members. For exchange officers from other services performing duties within AF units, the evaluation framework—whether AF forms, home-service forms, or both—is not clearly delineated between the two publications.
**Resolution:** AFI 36-2122 should include a dedicated section on evaluation procedures for exchange officers and cross-reference AFI 36-2406, or AFI 36-2406 should explicitly address exchange officer evaluation handling.

### Finding 12 — Career Broadening vs. First Sergeant Special Duty Assignment Interaction [LOW]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2112 (MMECBP) vs. AFI 36-2113 (The First Sergeant)
**Issue:** AFI 36-2112 establishes a 3-year career broadening program for 2S0X1 enlisted members, while AFI 36-2113 governs the first sergeant special duty program. Both involve temporary reclassification or special duty utilization of enlisted members but do not cross-reference each other. An enlisted member could theoretically be eligible for both programs simultaneously, and there is no guidance on precedence or deferral.
**Resolution:** Both publications should include cross-references establishing that participation in one program defers eligibility for the other, or that concurrent selection is resolved by the member's chain of command.

### Finding 13 — DAFPD 36-26 as Parent Directive for Multiple Publications [LOW]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2112 (MMECBP) vs. AFH 36-2647 (Competency Modeling) vs. AFGM 2026-36-2033 (Warrant Officers, partial)
**Issue:** DAFPD 36-26 (Total Force Development) is cited as the implementing directive by both AFI 36-2112 and AFH 36-2647, and is referenced by AFGM 2026-36-2033 (which implements multiple DAFPDs including 36-26). All three address force development from different angles—career broadening, competency modeling, and warrant officer development—but do not acknowledge each other's existence or establish how they collectively implement DAFPD 36-26.
**Resolution:** DAFPD 36-26 should include or reference a policy integration matrix listing all implementing publications and their respective scopes.

### Finding 14 — Warrant Officer Accession Age Authority [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFGM 2026-36-2033 (Warrant Officers)
**Issue:** The AFGM grants NGB/CF authority to "further restrict maximum accession age" for ANG warrant officers (§4.1.5), but does not grant equivalent authority to AF/RE for AFR warrant officers. This asymmetry could create a situation where ANG and AFR have different accession age standards for the same warrant officer career fields without clear policy rationale, and no other publication in the corpus addresses this gap.
**Resolution:** The permanent warrant officer instruction should either grant AF/RE equivalent authority or explain the policy rationale for the asymmetry. Accession standards should be harmonized across components where possible.

### Finding 15 — Space Force Exclusion Inconsistency [LOW]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-2109 vs. AFI 36-2113 vs. AFI 36-2135 vs. AFI 36-2406 vs. AFH 36-2647 vs. AFI 36-2122
**Issue:** Multiple publications include explicit "does not apply to the United States Space Force" language, while others (AFI 36-2112, AFI 36-2129, AFI 36-1301) do not include such a statement. This inconsistency creates ambiguity about whether the omission means the publication does apply to USSF or whether the exclusion was simply not stated.
**Resolution:** All 36-series publications should adopt standardized applicability language regarding USSF, preferably driven by a DAFPD-level template.

### Finding 16 — Tenure Management Overlap [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-1301 (Acquisition KLPs) vs. AFI 36-2113 (The First Sergeant)
**Issue:** Both publications establish tenure requirements for special positions—AFI 36-1301 for acquisition KLPs (with SAE-approved tenure periods and early release waivers) and AFI 36-2113 for first sergeants (with SDM-approved extensions beyond four years). Both create independent tenure management frameworks with separate waiver authorities, yet both affect the same personnel system and assignment processes managed by AFPC. There is no integrated tenure management policy.
**Resolution:** A cross-cutting tenure management policy should be established at the AF/A1 level, or both publications should cross-reference a common set of tenure management principles.

### Finding 17 — Acquisition KLP Coding vs. Joint Duty Position Coding [MEDIUM]
**Pubs:** AFI 36-1301 (Acquisition KLPs) vs. AFI 36-2135 (Joint Officer Management)
**Issue:** AFI 36-1301 requires MAJCOMs to ensure KLPs are "correctly coded in manpower and personnel systems" (§1.4.7), while AFI 36-2135 requires AF/A1M to ensure the "Air Force Manpower Programming and Execution System is updated bi-annually to reflect JDA positions." Both require position coding in the same manpower systems but establish separate update cycles and authorities. Positions that are simultaneously KLPs and JDAs could receive conflicting coding instructions.
**Resolution:** AF/A1M should serve as the single authority for position coding standards, with AFI 36-1301 and AFI 36-2135 both deferring to a common coding protocol.

### Finding 18 — AFGM Supremacy Clause and Existing Evaluation/Promotion Policy [HIGH]
**Pubs:** AFGM 2026-36-2033
